title: Macroptilium longepedunculatum
taxonomy: Macroptilium longepedunculatum, a tropical legume, is classified in the kingdom Plantae, phylum Magnoliophyta, class Magnoliopsida, order Fabales, family Fabaceae, and subfamily Faboideae. It belongs to the tribe Phaseoleae. The genus Macroptilium is a member of the Fabaceae family, which consists of pea and bean plants. This classification places Macroptilium longepedunculatum within the broader group of flowering plants. The subfamily Faboideae is characterized by its distinctive flower and seed characteristics.
uses: Macroptilium longepedunculatum, also known as the hairy stick or Jacob's stick, is a plant species native to Central and South America. The medicinal uses of Macroptilium longepedunculatum include treating various health issues such as fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. Its leaves and stems are used in traditional medicine to create teas and infusions, which are believed to have anti-inflammatory properties. Additionally, the plant is used to treat digestive problems and as a remedy for respiratory issues. Further research is needed to fully understand the potential medicinal benefits and effects of Macroptilium longepedunculatum.

intro: Macroptilium longepedunculatum, also known as the long-peduncled horse bean or scarlet runner bean, is a climbing legume native to tropical and subtropical regions of the Americas. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, which include alleviating symptoms of diabetes, fever, and inflammation, due to its potential health benefits such as hypoglycemic and anti-inflammatory effects. The bioactive constituents of Macroptilium longepedunculatum include alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, the plant is commonly prepared as an infusion or decoction to treat various ailments, and it is also used as a tisane or tea.
